entry.title: Re: Payment Terms Limitation for Installment Payments Abo...
entry.content:
Hi Priscilla Dias,

Number of Days field in Payment Terms is a standard SAP field and supports a maximum value of 999 days. This limitation is part of the standard product design and cannot be extended through configuration.

For payment terms that require due dates beyond 999 days, SAP recommends using the Fixed Day and Additional Months fields. These fields allow the system to determine the due date by adding the required number of months to the baseline date and, if required, setting a fixed day within the resulting month.

Therefore, for a 60-month installment scenario where the due date extends beyond 999 days, the recommended approach is to use Additional Months (and Fixed Day, where applicable) rather than relying on the Number of Days field. There is no standard SAP functionality available to increase the 999-day limit of the Number of Days field.

Best Regards,
Jyothi

entry.title: Error when using BSUoM on MM to do a outbound task
entry.content:
We work with materials whose basic unit of measure is piece, and we also use an alternative product unit of measure (BSUoM). The 2025 version allows only one batch characteristic.

We have a sales scenario where the sales unit is different from the basic unit of measure, using multiple alternative units. This scenario is possible in SAP 2020, where EWM works only with the basic unit of measure.

Has anyone encountered this issue in SAP 2023 or 2025? What was the solution?

An additional point is that we have the new "Batch Specification UoM" flag in EWM, which can be activated or deactivated via SPRO. However, when it is deactivated, it creates queues in the delivery integration with EWM. When it is activated, only deliveries using the basic unit of measure or a single alternative unit can be integrated.

Can anyone help?

entry.title: Creation and Setup of SAP Dynamic Forms from scratch
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am new to this sap dynamic form having a below requirement please guide to how to do setup and create my 1st dynamic form from scratch also please mention the pre-requisites needed for the same .

Requirement Explanation: Users should be able to access a Job Card form directly from the Work Order application, where they can capture all relevant job-related details such as tasks performed, activities completed, checklist confirmations, logos, signatures, and images. The form should also allow users to manage supporting documents by uploading, viewing, and deleting files as needed. Once the data is entered, it should be saved and linked to the respective Work Order, ensuring all information is centrally stored and easily retrievable. Additionally, a separate application should be available to view the saved Job Card information in a clear, structured, and user-friendly format for reference and reporting.

Question: How can we design and implement this requirement using SAP Dynamic Forms to ensure flexibility, minimal development effort, and an intuitive user experience for capturing and displaying Job Card data? Also, if this solution needs to be accessed via a mobile application, will any additional configuration or setup be required to ensure proper responsiveness, usability, and performance across devices?

 

Thanks,

Susmita

entry.title: Start Your SAP Career on Coursera: Two Certificates to Get You There
entry.content:
Looking to start a career in SAP but not sure where to begin? You’re not alone and there’s now a clear, accessible path. Nearly 200 million learners are already using Coursera to start or change their careers, and SAP is part of that journey. 

Coursera offers two SAP Professional Certificates, designed for accessibility and real career momentum. Self-paced, available in over 25 languages, needing no prior SAP experience, and completable in as little as 12 weeks. 

They are built for early-career professionals, students, and anyone looking to transition into SAP-related roles, offering a structured way to build confidence before stepping into the ecosystem.   

Each Coursera certificate targets a distinct role in the SAP ecosystem: 

  • SAP Technology Consultant Professional Certificate: Builds the core consulting skills SAP customers and partners look for, from requirements gathering and stakeholder management to solution design and navigating full SAP implementation lifecycles using SAP Activate. 
  • SAP Business Analyst Professional Certificate: Focuses on business analysis fundamentals, process modelling with SAP Signavio, data analysis with SAP Analytics Cloud, and how to bridge the gap between business requirements and technical delivery. 

Both are available on Coursera and carry ACE® (American Council on Education) credit recommendations. This means learners may be able to apply their learning toward academic credit at participating U.S. and Latin American universities. 

 The pathway to SAP Certification 

 These certificates don’t stop at foundational knowledge; they open the door to what comes next.  From there, learners can deepen their skills on SAP Learning site through self-paced courses and expert-led learning journeys. 

Ready to take the first step? Whether you’re starting your own SAP journey or supporting the next generation of talent, these certificates offer a practical and proven entry point into the ecosystem.

entry.title: Re: The Financial Statement Version is tied to a released...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Resolving Transport Request Issues for Financial Statement Version Activation

When you encounter the error message ‘Task BEMK900345 is not in status modifiable’ while attempting to activate a Financial Statement Version (FSV), it indicates that the task is part of a transport request that has already been released. To resolve this issue, follow these steps:

  1. Create a New Transport Request: Ensure that you have created a new transport request, which you have already done.

  2. Assign the New Transport Request: Unfortunately, the system does not automatically allow you to select a new transport request when editing the FSV. You need to manually assign the new transport request to the changes you are making.

  3. Use the Correct Application: Use the app ‘Manage Financial Statement Versions’  to make changes to the FSV. After saving the changes, the system should prompt you to activate the FSV, allowing you to select the new transport request.

  4. Check Transport Request Status: Ensure that the new transport request (BEMK900346) and task (BEMK900347) are in a modifiable status. If they are not, you may need to create another transport request or check with your system administrator.

  5. Activate the FSV: Once the new transport request is correctly assigned, attempt to activate the FSV again.

For more details, see the following information:

Knowledge Base Article: 3399551 - Financial Statement Version Hierarchy not Transport to P system - SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Financial Statement Version

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: SAP BPA automation: get token and pass to an API
entry.content:
Hi @shruti_chhabra ,

Ideally adding an await befor the api call should have worked.
Also, for fetching the token, it should be a GETcall and not a POST. 

const response = await irpa_core.request.call(options1); 

If you want to use the response later in automation, define an output parameter in your custom script and return the data in the variable as below.

    return {
        apiResponse: response
    }

Generally, 401 error is for unauthorized access. Seems your user does not have proper authorization for making the call or password is not correct.

Thanks,
Vaibhav

entry.title: Re: Error when starting submethod -
entry.content:
Despite your question being from 2011, I can answer it. Every time, you have the full amount of SAP sessions (6 by default) active (RZ11 - parameter "rdisp/max_alt_modes") this message is triggered when you try to run a workitem by double clicking on that workitem in transaction SBWP. If the underlying workflow task is part of a workflow and if the workflow step has a side method implemented, there is no free session available for the side method to be executed.

Before SAP introduced OSS note707636 https://me.sap.com/notes/707636 in 2004 there was not even a message about this.

Give it a try: open the maximum amount of SAP sessions within SAP GUI for windows until this message appears:

Start your workitem from transaction SBWP which has a side method implemented in the workflow step and the error occures:

The woritem opens its task but it does not open the side method's task.

entry.title: Re: Request for Updated Crystal Reports Runtime (.NET 64-...
entry.content:
Often those CVE's can be reported but CR Products do not use the internal API that is affected.

I've passed it on to R&D to look into.

In the mean time you can add an exception, R&D won't update the version just because if it doesn't affect CR products.

PS - All of BOE/CRS/CR products use the same runtime so it's not simple to replace one file. Requires QA'ing all of them.

Also note CR for VS does not release patches, full builds only, next release won't be until end of year or January 2027.

In the mean time you may have to go back to a previous version before the reported issue.

entry.title: Re: Unable to assign new GL account to custom movement ty...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Solution for Custom Movement Type Account Determination

To address the issue of not seeing the general modification ‘ZBR’ in the dropdown for automatic account determination, you can consider the following options:

  1. Create a Custom Access Sequence:

    • You can create a custom access sequence by copying the standard access sequence  and adding your custom general modification ‘ZBR’. This allows you to define specific account determination rules for your custom movement type. For more information, see Change Account Determination Access Sequence - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition,

  2. Enhancement Options:

    • If creating a custom access sequence does not resolve the issue, you may need to explore enhancement options. This could involve custom development to modify the account determination logic to recognize the ‘ZBR’ modifier.

If none of these options work for you, please let us know.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Improved Data Action scoping with the new Data Filter Parameter type
entry.content:
Overview

Designing powerful planning and analytics processes requires more than static member selections. In many real-world scenarios, planning users rely on dynamic story filters and input controls, including those with exclusions, time ranges, and attribute-based filtering to scope of their data updates and reporting activities.  Up until now, however, these advanced filter selection scenarios could be defined in the story but could not be fully leveraged when executing data actions. Rather, consumption of story filters and input controls in data actions was restricted to those filters using explicit member lists (or the All member). 

To cover dynamic selection scenarios designers often had to use workarounds which necessitated significant maintenance or rely on the users to select appropriate members based on a static list.  In many cases, this would lead to inconsistencies between what users saw or selected in a story and the scope of execution processed by the data action, with the net effect of processing of more data than should be required.  Enter the data filter parameter.

 

Introducing the Data Filter Parameter

With the introduction of data filter parameters (releasing with our Q3/2026 QRC), SAP Analytics Cloud takes a major step forward in making data actions’ consumption of parameter values more flexible, consistent, and aligned with the way users work in stories.  Data filter parameters help to reconcile what the user selects and visualizes (including dynamic filter selections) with what the data action processes.   It represents a new parameter type that allows any number of dimension filters to be passed as-is into a single parameter, while allowing the filter selection to cover to dynamic story filters and input controls, including support for:

  • Exclusions (“everything except…”)
  • Dynamic date ranges or custom current date selections
  • Attribute based dimension filters

Instead of resolving an individual static member-list for each dimension that needs to be filtered and passing those to separate parameters, data actions (and multi action executing data action steps) can now capture the filter definition from the story or input control while preserving its full semantics, and do so across multiple dimensions within a single parameter.

 

Configuring Data Filter Parameters

Data filter parameters are configured like other parameters within the data action, but they differ from other dimension-based parameters in that the parameter itself is dynamic.  Specifically, the design-time does not involve specifying which dimensions, elements, or filters will be passed to the parameter (there is an exception to this for Advanced Formulas, which we will discuss). Rather, that is determined in story design time based on the specific input controls and/or story filters assigned to the parameter within a given data action (or multi action) starter.   In this way, the parameter itself is much more flexible than traditional dimension-based parameters, allowing it scale to filter a single dimension selection or any number of dimensions (including support for multiple filters on the same dimension), while also allowing these filters to differ completely based on the specific calling trigger.

Figure 1

Figure 2

As with other dimension-based parameters, data filter parameters are applied in the context area of data action step types, including:

  • Copy
  • Cross-model copy
  • Currency conversion
  • Deletion
  • Structured allocation

In these scenarios, the filter acts as a precise scoping mechanism, determining which data is read and processed while retaining the full target logic and calculation capabilities of the step.

Figure 3

In Advanced Formula steps, two new configuration options are available:

  1. CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_PARAMETERS – used to activate one or more filter parameters to be used in the Advanced Formula step
  2. CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_DIMENSIONS - to define exactly which dimensions from the filter apply should apply and can be used in place of MEMBERSET to cover dimension filtering 

Note that while the other steps will automatically filter based on all dimensions fed into the parameter in the story trigger, Advanced Formulas require explicit definition of some, or all, of the included dimensions via the CONFIG.DATA_FILTER_DIMENSIONS setting.   Reference to explicit data filter-parameter dimensions/elements can also occur via DATA(), DELETE(), and RESULTLOOKUP statements within the Advanced Formula. However, where this occurs, the explicit configuration option CONFIG.HIERARCHY also needs to be set for those dimensions. 

Figure 4

Summary

The data filter parameter reflects SAP’s ongoing investment in aligning analysis and planning actions within a single, consistent user experience. By allowing data actions (and multi actions that call them) to consume an expanded array of dynamic story filters and do so within a single parameter whose scope can vary based on the specific calling instance, data filter parameters help simplify data action design, use, and performance.  As planning scenarios continue to grow in complexity, this enhancement ensures that data actions scale with the sophistication of SAP Analytics Cloud stories—without sacrificing clarity or control.

entry.title: Simplifying SAP AI Agent Architecture for Beginners - #1 High Level Overview
entry.content:
SAP has recently released a detailed reference architecture for AI Agents at SAP Architecture Center.

These reference architectures are great. However, for a beginner this could be a bit overwhelming to understand.

In this blog series, we will try to simplify the SAP AI Agent architecture, how the entire call flow works, how Joule Agents interact with 3rd-party agents, how the custom agent works etc.

The #1 blog focuses on…

High-Level AI Agent Architecture

                                                                   Ref: SAP Architecture Center

 

1. Joule at the Center

Joule is the central orchestrator and entry point for all user interactions.

Joule UI
Joule UI is where users interacts with Joule and enter the prompt.

Joule Orchestrator
Joule’s planning and reasoning engine determines the best way to fulfill it.
Joule orchestrator decides whether the request should be handled by:
• A Joule Skill
• A Joule Agent
• A knowledge retrieval process
• A custom agent

Joule Capabilities
Joule’s common capabilities are Informational, Transactional, Navigational and Analytical.
Joule orchestrator decides which capabilities to be used or delegate the task to an agent.

Joule Skill and Joule Agents
A Joule Skill is a reusable business function that performs a specific task when invoked by Joule. For example, Create Purchase Order or Approve Leave Request.

A Joule Agent is an AI agent. It can plan, reason, and execute multiple actions to achieve a business goal.

For example user may ask “Onboard a new employee joining next Monday”.

In this case, the agent may

  • Create employee record
  • Assign laptop
  • Request access permissions
  • Schedule orientation
  • Notify manager etc.

A user’s request may involve using a built-in skill or retrieving information or delegating the task to an AI agent.

 

2. Custom Agents on SAP BTP

SAP supports two complementary paths for building AI agents.

1. Joule Studio based Agents
Build agents using Joule Studio using intent-based development

For Joule Studio Based Agents, the platform handles the integration work. When a user’s prompt matches the agent’s purpose, Joule invokes the corresponding agent.

 

2. SAP Cloud SDK for AI based Agents
Develop agents using Python or TypeScript with full programmatic control. SAP Cloud SDK for AI provides seamless integration with Generative AI Hub and AI Core.
These agents run in their own independent runtime environments and are integrated with Joule using the open A2A protocol.

 

3. MCP Gateway in Integration Suite

MCP Gateway is going to be a new capability within SAP Integration Suite.

Customers may use MCP Gateway to expose SAP and non-SAP APIs as an MCP server.

It would also help customers, create, manage and expose their own MCP servers along with lifecycle management, governance and monitoring.

For example, a customer needs to expose Purchase Order API from S/4HANA. They may use MCP Gateway to expose these APIs as MCP tools for AI agents.

Check the roadmap to know about its availability.

 

4. Agent Gateway

Agent Gateway that enables external clients and applications to consume Joule Agents through standardized protocols.

The Agent Gateway exposes Joule Agents via the A2A protocol with an externally reachable endpoint. This enables third-party AI Agents, applications, partner systems and custom business applications to consume SAP-native agents.

For example, using Agent Gateway, 3rd party platform like Microsoft Copilot Studio can delegate SAP-specific tasks to Joule Agents.

The Agent Gateway is not yet generally available.

 

2 Important Concepts to Revise

1. Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol
The Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol is an open standard for communication and collaboration between autonomous AI agents.

A2A protocol enables one agent to delegate tasks to another, inquire about its capabilities and exchange information in a structured manner.

2. Model Context Protocol (MCP)
MCP is an open standard that defines how AI models and agents can discover, understand and interact with external tools.

MCP acts as a universal adapter, allowing agents to consume tools without needing to know their underlying implementation details.

 

What’s Next?

In the next blog, we will simplify the end-to-end call flow… and see what really happens after a user submits a prompt.

Disclaimer: This blog reflects my personal understanding, interpretation, and point of view.

entry.title: Re: Dynamic Parameter needs credentials
entry.content:
You are correct...

For Crystal to display the list of values it needs to connect to the DB to populate the dialog box with the values to select from.

CR fully supports Database security, you don't want to give access to sensitive data if the user doesn't have rights to see the data.

Talk to the ERP developers for work arounds by possibly creating a User group with permissions to access that data in the DB.

entry.title: Optimizing Industrial Water Consumption at Scale
entry.content:
From EHS Compliance Management to Decision Enablement with SAP Business Data Cloud

 1.     Introduction

Water scarcity is no longer a distant sustainability concern. It is a direct operational constraint for many industrial companies. In manufacturing environments, water availability and regulatory limits increasingly influence how production can be planned and executed.

In this context, water management goes far beyond compliance reporting. It directly determines which assets can operate, which processes must be adapted and which investments need to be prioritized. In addition, water usage is directly associated with operational costs, meaning that reducing water consumption can also contribute to measurable cost savings. Regulatory requirements are actively enforced, meaning that exceeding permitted limits can result in fines or operational restrictions. At the same time, many organizations still manage environmental and operational data in disconnected systems. This limits visibility into consumption drivers at plant and asset level and makes structured decision-making difficult.

This blog post presents a use case that shows how SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Environment, Health & Safety (EHS), together with the SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and SAP Project and Portfolio Management (PPM), enables organizations to move from pure compliance monitoring to structured, decision-driven water management.

2.     Sustainability & Compliance as Drivers for Operational Decisions

Water-related regulations are becoming more dynamic and more restrictive. In practice, this means that production sites must continuously ensure that actual water withdrawal remains within permitted limits defined by local authorities.

This requirement directly impacts operations. It influences whether certain assets can run at full capacity, whether processes need to be adjusted, and which mitigation measures must be implemented to stay within legal boundaries.

Managing this effectively requires connecting regulatory requirements with operational execution. Permits and environmental limits defined in EHS must be closely linked to asset-level consumption within the plant structure, while decision-making must also consider feasibility, timing and operational impact. This is where the interaction between EHS, EAM and BDC becomes essential.

 3.     EHS as the Foundation for Environmental Data

SAP EHS plays a central role by acting as the system of record for regulatory-compliant environmental data. It does not simply collect data, but ensures that environmental information is validated, traceable, and consistently aligned with regulatory requirements.

In this scenario, EHS is used to manage water withdrawal permits at plant level, maintain legally binding environmental limits and structure data collection across assets. In addition, it provides calculated KPIs such as equipment-level water consumption, evaporation and wastewater discharge. As a result, organizations gain a reliable and auditable basis for demonstrating compliance, ensuring that environmental performance is not only understood, but can also be proven at any point in time.

At the same time, operating within regulatory constraints requires continuous decision-making. As conditions change, organizations must determine how to adjust operations and which measures to prioritize in order to remain compliant.

4.     From Compliance to Decision-Making

Building on the regulatory foundation provided by EHS, organizations are required to take decisions under real operational constraints. In practice, this involves evaluating multiple mitigation measures across different assets, each with distinct impacts on water consumption, operational performance and cost.

While EHS ensures that compliance requirements are clearly defined and reliably monitored, additional capabilities are required to compare these measures in a structured way. Decision-making in this context is not about identifying a single issue, but about evaluating several possible actions and understanding their consequences.

Extending the EHS foundation with an integrated decision layer allows organizations to move beyond transparency. It allows them to compare measures, assess their impact across environmental and financial dimensions and determine how to best maintain compliance under changing conditions.

5.     EHS Data Model & Setup

Within EHS, water consumption is modeled at a detailed level across the plant and asset structure. Data is collected for different water sources such as purchased water, rainfall, well water and linked to process usage such as cooling and cleaning.

These data points are transformed into validated and calculated KPIs, including equipment water consumption, wastewater discharge, evaporation and water loss. A central analytical concept is the water balance, which connects water sources, consumption patterns and environmental outcomes. This provides transparency on how water flows through the production system and highlights which assets are the main drivers of consumption and inefficiency.

The following diagram illustrates an example of such a water balance, showing typical input and output flows within a production environment. It is important to note that this represents a simplified scenario and additional input and output types can be configured depending on the specific requirements of a production site.

This type of visualization makes it possible to trace water from its origin through consumption and processing steps to its final outcome, supporting both detailed analysis and intuitive interpretation of complex relationships.

6.     Demo Use Case – From Regulation to Decision Pressure

The use case is based on the fictional company Dglue, a global manufacturer operating multiple production facilities. At its Bremen site, Production Hall B operates several water-intensive assets, including reactors, mixers and extrusion systems.

Regulatory Trigger: Due to severe drought conditions, local authorities introduce a temporary restriction, reducing the permitted daily water withdrawal from 760 m³ to 650 m³—a reduction of 15%.

Operational Impact: This restriction has an immediate effect on production, particularly on water-intensive cooling processes. Without intervention, the site faces a significant risk of exceeding regulatory limits, which could lead to production disruptions. As a result, compliance is no longer a purely reporting-related task, but becomes a critical operational constraint.

The Resulting Decision Problem: The plant manager is therefore confronted with a set of concrete decisions. It must be determined whether certain assets need to be adjusted or temporarily reduced, which combination of mitigation measures can bring the site back within permitted limits and how operational disruption can be minimized.

Different options may lead to similar compliance outcomes, but with significantly different impacts on production and cost. This transforms the situation into a multi-dimensional decision problem under regulatory constraints, where alternatives must be evaluated and prioritized.

7.     From Data to Decisions: The Role of Business Data Cloud

The SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) builds on the EHS foundation and enables organizations to translate transparency into structured decision-making.

Rather than focusing on data integration alone, BDC provides the ability to evaluate and compare alternative mitigation scenarios. It supports the analysis of planned, forecasted and actual outcomes over time and connects environmental KPIs with operational and cost-related perspectives. In practical terms, this allows organizations to assess how different measures perform relative to each other. Mitigation options can be compared not only based on their impact on water consumption, but also with regard to implementation cost and operational implications. In this role, BDC acts as a decision-enablement layer, allowing organizations to determine which combination of measures is most effective in maintaining compliance while minimizing operational and financial impact. This highlights that the core challenge is not access to data, but the ability to make structured and comparable decisions based on that data.

 8.     End-to-End Process Flow

The integration of EHS and BDC enables a closed-loop process that connects compliance monitoring, analytics, decision-making and execution. In practice, this process reflects how plant managers and environmental managers interact with the system to ensure compliance under real operational constraints, typically triggered by a compliance risk or a forecasted exceedance of permitted limits.

1. Compliance Targets & Measurement (EHS)

Environmental limits and permits are defined and monitored using structured EHS data collections and calculations. Water consumption is captured at plant and asset level, establishing full transparency over actual usage. This creates the compliance baseline from which all further analysis and decisions are derived.

The configuration of compliance requirements and data collection within SAP EHS can be illustrated through the following screens. They show how environmental limits, data collection structures and measurement values are defined and maintained at plant and asset level.

This setup ensures that all relevant data is captured consistently and forms the basis for reliable compliance monitoring and further analytics. The setup of compliance requirements and water balance logic in SAP EHS follows a structured configuration approach. A detailed step-by-step guide on how to configure water balance and usage reporting within SAP can be found in the official SAP blog post.

2. Analytics Dashboard (BDC + SAC)

EHS data is integrated into BDC and visualized using SAP Analytics Cloud, providing a real-time view of water consumption across the plant.

Users can interact directly with the dashboard by filtering data across locations, equipment and time periods. This allows them to analyze trends, compare consumption across assets and identify which processes are driving water usage.

In addition to standard KPI monitoring, users can leverage advanced capabilities such as ad-hoc queries and AI-supported insights to better understand underlying drivers and potential optimization areas.

3. Define Mitigation Measures (BDC-Triggered)

Based on these insights, potential mitigation measures are identified and evaluated.

At this stage, the process evolves from analysis to structured decision-making. Measures are not treated as isolated ideas but are formalized as project demands within Project Portfolio Management (PPM). This simulation view allows decision-makers to directly compare the impact of different measures, making trade-offs between water savings, implementation cost and operational implications transparent.

Each project demand captures key attributes such as expected water savings, associated implementation costs and operational implications. This allows decision-makers to directly compare alternatives and evaluate trade-offs between environmental impact and financial effort.

Different scenarios can be analyzed to determine which combination of measures is most effective in bringing the plant back within regulatory limits while minimizing operational disruption.

4. Implementation & Operational Adjustments

Once a decision has been made, selected measures are implemented through standard operational processes, including maintenance activities or larger transformation initiatives within EAM and PPM. At this stage, decisions are translated into concrete operational actions.

5. Continuous Monitoring (BDC + SAC)

After implementation, actual performance is continuously monitored and compared with planned targets.

This enables early identification of deviations and allows organizations to adjust measures if required.

The result is a fully integrated feedback loop from data to decision to action and back to monitoring, ensuring that compliance is continuously maintained and optimized over time.

9.     System Architecture: Why This Combination Matters

The value of the solution lies in the interaction between its components. SAP EHS provides regulatory correctness, data validation and auditability, ensuring that all environmental data is compliant and trustworthy. SAP Analytics Cloud enables analytical visibility and supports root cause analysis, while SAP EAM and Project Portfolio Management support the execution of operational and investment-related measures.

SAP Business Data Cloud connects these layers and enables structured evaluation across systems. It ensures that compliance data, operational realities and decision-making logic are brought together into a consistent and integrated view. In addition, BDC leverages standardized data products, which reduce implementation effort through ready-to-use, harmonized data models. These data products can be easily connected and extended, supporting a clean-core approach and enabling organizations to integrate additional data sources without disrupting existing system landscapes.

The interaction between these components can be illustrated through the overall solution architecture:

Individually, each component fulfills a specific role. However, on its own, none of them is sufficient to support end-to-end decision-making. EHS provides compliance transparency but does not enable structured comparison of mitigation measures. Analytics tools provide insights but lack the regulatory grounding required for compliance decisions. Execution systems enable action but do not provide the necessary prioritization logic. In addition, BDC supports advanced analytical capabilities such as predictive modeling and AI-driven insights, further enhancing the evaluation of alternative scenarios.

Only the combination of these components enables organizations to connect compliance, analysis, decision-making and execution into a coherent process. This allows for decision-driven operations within regulatory constraints, where environmental, operational and financial dimensions can be considered together.

 10.     Embedding Sustainability into Decisions

By integrating project demands into the process, sustainability becomes an integral part of operational planning rather than a separate reporting activity.

Organizations can evaluate alternative measures before implementation, compare their impact and prioritize those that provide the most effective balance between water reduction, operational performance and associated cost.

This makes trade-offs explicit and allows decision-makers to assess both the environmental benefit and the financial and operational implications of each option.

As a result, sustainability is no longer treated as an isolated KPI but becomes a core decision criterion embedded directly into day-to-day operations.

11.     Lessons Learned

One of the key learnings from this scenario is the importance of a regulatory-grade data foundation. Without consistent, validated and traceable data, meaningful analysis and reliable decision-making are not possible.

In addition, clearly defined ownership across roles such as Environmental Manager and Plant Manager is essential to ensure that insights are effectively translated into action.

Finally, a clean-core architecture provides the necessary flexibility and scalability, enabling the solution to be extended to additional sustainability domains and evolving regulatory requirements over time.

 12.     Conclusion

Managing industrial water consumption at scale is no longer only about maintaining compliance. It is about enabling effective decisions under regulatory constraints.

SAP EHS provides the trusted and auditable foundation required to ensure compliance. When complemented by SAP Business Data Cloud, this data becomes actionable, enabling organizations to evaluate how to respond to changing conditions. By enabling the structured comparison of mitigation options through project demands, organizations can systematically assess trade-offs between water reduction, cost and operational impact. This allows decisions to be made not only from a compliance perspective, but based on a balanced evaluation of environmental, operational and financial considerations.

In this way, sustainability evolves from a reporting obligation into a core capability for decision-driven operations.

---

Engage with us

To follow the series, make sure to subscribe to the SAP EHS Public Cloud blog tag.

We welcome your thoughts and feedback in the comments!

Further Reading

For readers interested in the technical configuration of water balance and usage reporting in SAP EHS, the following step-by-step guide provides a detailed explanation.

Acknowledgements

This blog post series is the result of close collaboration within our EHS implementation team. I would like to express my sincere appreciation to my colleagues, whose expertise, insights and project experience significantly contributed to the depth and quality of this guide.

A special thank you goes to:

  • @Westphal 
  • @duynguyendeloitte 
  • @kpoeschk 
  • @Prakash_Kamaraj 

 Their contributions were instrumental in shaping the content of this blog post. I am truly grateful for their collaborations and support.

entry.title: Re: Question about a potential bug in crystal report 2026
entry.content:
Need specific info...

What Database Server are you connecting to?

How are you connecting, OLE DB, ODBC, Native client etc.?

The problem could be due to Client updates, changes between 32 bit Clients and 64 bit Clients, out of CR's control.

Note: CR 2020 is a 32 bit application and CR 2025 is a 64 bit application.

As you noted the only way for CR to "fix" the differences is for you to manually Verify the reports database connection info. 

CR has a copy of the DB connection saved in the RPT file, so it does need to update the info when changes are done.

entry.title: Automated SQL Plan Management to recover from degraded performance
entry.content:
You apply an upgrade over the weekend. Monday morning, someone reports that a critical reporting query is running much slower. You dig into the plan cache, compare before/after execution plans, find the optimizer chose a different join order, capture the old plan, pin it — and hours later, things are back to normal.

SAP HANA Cloud's SQL Plan Advisor, combined with SQL Plan Stability, now supports a fully automated detect-test-apply cycle that handles this class of degraded performance without manual intervention. Here is how it works and how to configure it.

The Two Pieces You Need to Know

SQL Plan Stability — Your Insurance Policy

SQL Plan Stability lets you capture an Abstract SQL Plan (ASP) for any SELECT statement. An ASP is a compact, engine-independent representation of an execution plan's key structural decisions: join order, join types, access paths, aggregation strategies. It is stored persistently and can be used later to regenerate that same physical plan, even after a software update or statistics refresh that would otherwise cause the optimizer to choose something different.

Key points:
- SAP HANA Cloud now automatically collects ASP for every distinct HEX plans and corresponding statistics.
- For non-HEX, only the statistics are collected for the corresponding statement hash

As ASPs are collected for every compiled plans with its corresponding statistics, we have a clearer understanding of what is a good plan versus a bad plan. This information is used it to protect against future degradation.

SQL Plan Advisor — The Automated Testing Engine

SQL Plan Advisor is the active component. It continuously monitors SQL execution, detects performance degradation by comparing the current execution plans statistics over the statistics stored in SQL Plan Stability, and now  automatically runs A/B tests between the current plan and candidate alternatives to find and validate a better one.

The candidate plan types it evaluates are:

Candidate TypeWhat It Is
NON_HEXA plan generated by the non-HEX execution engine, tested when the HEX plan degrades
CAPTURED_ABSTRACT_SQL_PLANAn ASP previously captured via SQL Plan Stability
PLAN_VARIANT

An alternative plan from the plan cache, typically tied to a different parameter binding by caching multiple plans for a given statement hash

** NOT AUTOMATICALLY TESTED **

 

For parameterised queries, it is first determined if it can be covered by Plan Variant based on selectivity. If covered, the SQL Plan Advisor can test and collect statistics over the current plan and 50 with each alternative and checks for deviance before issuing a recommendation. As the testing maybe extensive, Plan Variant is currently scoped out for the automated testing. 

 

The Fully Automated Flow

When configured for full automation, the pipeline looks like this:


Query executes
│
▼
HANA detects degradation (execution / CPU time compared to history)
│
▼
Statement auto-registered in SQL Plan Advisor
│
▼
Advisor tests current plan vs. candidates (NON_HEX, ASP)
over multiple live executions — no downtime, no test window
│
▼
Winner identified by empirical statistics
│
▼
Better plan auto-applied on the next execution
│
▼
Monitoring views updated — DBA can review at any time

The DBA is not in the critical path. You are informed after the fact, not paged during the incident.

 

Configuration

Step 1 — Enable SQL Plan Stability Capture for Critical Queries

Do this before your next update. In SAP HANA Cloud Central, navigate to SQL Plan Stability and enable capture for your most sensitive statements. For most instances, this should be already enabled.

Or use SQL directly:

-- Enable capture for a specific statement hash
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER SQL PLAN CACHE ENTRY '<statement_hash>'
SET PLAN STABILITY CAPTURE PLAN = TRUE;

Captured ASPs land in `M_SQL_PLAN_CACHE_EXECUTION_PLAN_STATISTICS` and are referenced in the `CAPTURED_ABSTRACT_SQL_PLAN` column of `M_SQL_PLAN_ADVISOR`.

Step 2 — Configure SQL Plan Advisor Parameters

In SAP HANA Cloud Central, navigate to SQL Plan Advisor and enable Register Queries, and set Test Queries and Apply Advice to Automatically which should be the default configuration

 

Monitoring

You can monitor all of this graphically in SAP HANA Cloud Central → SQL Plan Advisor, where the UI shows registered queries, test progress, performance comparisons, and applied recommendations in one view.

 

How SQL Plan Stability and SQL Plan Advisor Work Together

The two features are complementary, not redundant:

- SQL Plan Stability is reactive and defensive: capture a known-good plan, apply it when something goes wrong. It requires you to have captured an ASP beforehand.

- SQL Plan Advisor is proactive and exploratory: it detects degradation automatically, tests multiple candidates (including your captured ASPs), and applies the empirically best one.

The combined strategy:

1. Before a QRC update: Capture ASPs for your critical statements via SQL Plan Stability which should be already captured.
2. After the update: If the optimizer picks a worse plan for any of those statements, SQL Plan Advisor detects the degradation, tests the captured ASP against the new plan, and automatically applies the better one. Or, if a non-HEX plan is now converted to a HEX plan but the performance have degraded then reverts the execution back to non-HEX.
3. Ongoing: Advisor continues monitoring for new degradation across the full workload, not just the statements you pre-selected.

You get both a safety net (captured ASPs) and an autonomous recovery mechanism (Advisor auto-apply).

 

What Gets Automated vs. What Still Needs DBA Judgement

Fully automated:

- Detection of performance degradation
- Registration of degraded statements as test candidates
- A/B testing based on historical plans statistics. 
- Application of the most performant plan

 

Still requires DBA attention:

- Parameterized queries which can be covered by Plan Variant should be manually tested if Plan Variant is applicable.---

Conclusion

The automated testing was first applied with QRC 01/2026 version and we have been closely monitoring on a daily basis. Based on our telemetry data, 155 plans have been corrected back to a more performant plan based on historically captured ASPs. We have noticed up to 20 times performance increases after the recovery. Most of the reasons of degraded performance was due to statistics changes in the underly tables or plan changes for parameterized queries which couldn't be covered by Plan Variant.

We are planning to improve the coverage of SQL Plan Advisor and improve the usability of our tooling for better manage SQL plans and improve user experiences.

References

- SQL Plan Advisor — SAP Help Portal
- Automating SQL Plan Management with SQL Plan Advisor — SAP Learning
- Leveraging SQL Plan Stability for Performance Optimization — SAP Learning
- Protect from Performance Regression with SQL Plan Stability — SAP Community Blog
- Using SQL Plan Advisor in SAP HANA Cloud Central — SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Replicating SAP S/4HANA CDS View Entities into SAP HANA Cloud: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
In analytics, speed is everything—yet pulling real-time data from a live SAP S/4HANA system remains a bottleneck for many organizations. When your reports rely on remote queries, they are instantly vulnerable to network latency and cross-system traffic. CDS view replication changes the game by moving the data to where the processing happens: caching it directly into SAP HANA Cloud and automating background syncs for instant reporting.

 

This post walks through everything you need to know to set up, operate, and monitor CDS view replication using the SAP HANA smart data access **abapodbc** adapter which is an extension to the already available federation as introduced by my colleague SeungjoonLee and my previous blog.

 

Related Blogs:
  • Taking Data Federation to the Next Level: Accessing Remote ABAP CDS View Entities in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Easily create data snapshots from ABAP environment's CDS Views in SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database

 

What Is CDS View Entity Replication?

At its core, CDS view replication lets you maintain a local copy of an SAP S/4HANA CDS view as a native table inside SAP HANA Cloud. Once the initial snapshot is loaded, subsequent updates are applied using **Change Data Capture (CDC)** — only inserts, updates, and deletes are fetched and replayed, not the entire dataset.

The result is a target table in SAP HANA Cloud that stays in sync with your S/4HANA source, with a freshness you control.

Why This Matters

- Performance: Local queries beat remote queries — no cross-system round trips at runtime.
- Availability: Your replica stays accessible even if the S/4HANA system is under maintenance.
- Flexibility: You can project specific columns or filter rows at the subscription level, so you only replicate what you need.
- Integration: The replicated table is a plain SAP HANA table — you can join it with other local data, build calculation views on top of it, or expose it via BTP services.

 

The Two Replication Phases

Replication is split into two distinct phases:

Phase 1: Initial Load

The initial load takes a full snapshot of the CDS view and copies it into the target table. You trigger this with the `QUEUE` command. The system handles chunking and parallelism internally — you just monitor progress.

One important caveat: the initial load does **not** check whether the target table is empty. If you're reloading after a reset, truncate the target table first.

Phase 2: Delta Load

Once the initial load completes, only changes (inserts, updates, deletes) are replicated. You have two options here:

- System-scheduled replication (`DISTRIBUTE`): The system automatically runs delta replication at a configurable interval. Set it once, let it run.
- User-driven replication (`DELTA LOAD`): You explicitly trigger replication whenever you want. Useful for controlled refresh scenarios or testing.

 

Setting It Up: 

Pre-requisite A — Setup Source System

  • Enabling Access to ABAP-Managed Data for System-External Consumers
  • Accessing ABAP-Managed Data from System-External Consumers
    • Accessing ABAP-Managed Data from SAP HANA Cloud and SAP HANA

Pre-requisite B — Configure Cloud Connector to Source 

  • Configure Cloud Connector
  • Configure Access Control (RFC) in the SAP BTP Connectivity documentation

Step 1 — Create a Remote Source

You need an abapodbc adapter remote source that connects SAP HANA Cloud to your S/4HANA system. This is your network tunnel between the two systems.

Step 2 — Create the Virtual Table and Target Table

Create a virtual table from the remote source (this is your read-through proxy to the CDS view), and create a local target table that will hold the replicated data.

Step 3 — Create the Remote Subscription

This is where the replication is defined. The `CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION` statement ties everything together:

-- Simplest form: replicate everything
CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION"
ON "SOURCE_CDS_VIEW"
TARGET TABLE "TARGET_TABLE";

-- Column projection: only replicate specific fields
CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION"
AS (SELECT "COL1", "COL2", "COL3" FROM "SOURCE_CDS_VIEW")
TARGET TABLE "TARGET_TABLE";

-- Row filter: only replicate rows matching a condition
CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION"
AS (SELECT * FROM "SOURCE_CDS_VIEW" WHERE "DEPARTMENT" = 'SALES')
TARGET TABLE "TARGET_TABLE";

The `WHERE` clause supports `=`, `<>`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, and logical operators `AND`/`OR`/`NOT`. A few syntax rules apply: field names must be double-quoted, string values single-quoted, and the right-hand side of a comparison must be a literal (no field-to-field comparisons).

Step 4 — Perform the Initial Load

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" QUEUE;

Monitor progress in the `M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_INITIAL_LOADS` system view. The number of chunks can increase dynamically during the load as the system generates new ones.

Step 5 — Enable Delta Replication

Choose your mode:

-- Scheduled: run every 15 minutes
ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" DISTRIBUTE INTERVAL 900;

-- On-demand: trigger manually
ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" DELTA LOAD;

 

Load Behaviors: Normal vs. Upsert

When you create the subscription, you choose how DML operations are applied to the target table.

BehaviorINSERTUPDATEDELETE
NormalApplied directlyApplied directlyApplied directly
UpsertApplied as UPSERTApplied as UPSERTConverted to UPDATE with `CHANGE_TYPE = 'D'`

The upsert mode is useful when you want to preserve a history of deletions or need idempotent replay. It requires three additional metadata columns in the target table as shown below:

CREATE TABLE "TARGET_TABLE" (
"ID" INT,
"NAME" NVARCHAR(100),
"CHANGE_TYPE" NVARCHAR(1), -- 'A' = insert/update, 'D' = delete
"CHANGE_TIME" TIMESTAMP,
"CHANGE_SEQ" BIGINT -- optional: portion identifier
);

CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION"
ON "SOURCE_CDS_VIEW"
TARGET TABLE "TARGET_TABLE"
CHANGE TYPE COLUMN "CHANGE_TYPE"
CHANGE TIME COLUMN "CHANGE_TIME"
CHANGE SEQUENCE COLUMN "CHANGE_SEQ"
UPSERT;

 

Managing Replication Day-to-Day

Adjusting the Schedule

You can update the replication interval without triggering an immediate run — useful for shifting frequency during off-peak hours:

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" INTERVAL 1800; -- 30 minutes

To immediately trigger replication AND update the interval:

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" DISTRIBUTE INTERVAL 1800;

Suspending During Maintenance

If your S/4HANA system is going down for maintenance, suspend replication at the remote source level. All subscriptions under that source are paused; running jobs are allowed to finish but no new ones are scheduled.

ALTER REMOTE SOURCE <remote_source_name> SUSPEND CAPTURE;

-- When the system is back up:
ALTER REMOTE SOURCE <remote_source_name> RESUME CAPTURE;

Note that `SUSPEND CAPTURE` only affects system-scheduled jobs. Manual `DELTA LOAD` commands still work.

Cancelling a Running Job

If a replication job is consuming too many resources or is stuck, you can cancel it mid-execution:

-- Find the running job
SELECT CONNECTION_ID
FROM M_REMOTE_REPLICATION_JOBS
WHERE STATUS = 'RUNNING'
AND REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_NAME = 'ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION';

-- Cancel it
ALTER SYSTEM CANCEL SESSION '<connection_id>';

The system rolls back any partial changes, restoring the target table to its pre-job state. The job is automatically rescheduled for its next interval.

Resetting vs. Dropping

Reset returns the subscription to its initial state without removing it. Use this when you need to re-run the full initial load:

ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" RESET;
TRUNCATE TABLE "TARGET_TABLE";
ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION" QUEUE;

Drop permanently removes the subscription and cleans up resources on the ABAP source. The target table is retained.

DROP REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION "ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION";

 

Monitoring: The System Views You Need

SAP HANA provides a set of dedicated system views for CDS view replication. Here's when to use each one:

ViewUse Case
REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONSCheck subscription configuration, status, and the current replicated version (`EXTERNAL_VERSION_ID`)
M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONSRuntime health and status of active subscriptions
M_REMOTE_REPLICATION_JOBSFull lifecycle of scheduled jobs — PLANNED, RUNNING, COMPLETED, FAILED, CANCELLED
M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_INITIAL_LOADSInitial load progress by chunk: status, record count, execution time
M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS_ABAPODBCABAP-side replication ID and last processed portion ID

A few useful queries to keep handy:

-- Is my initial load done?
SELECT CHUNK_ID, TOTAL_RECORD_COUNT, EXECUTION_STATUS, EXECUTION_TIME
FROM M_REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_INITIAL_LOADS
WHERE REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_NAME = 'ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION';

-- What's my latest replicated version?
SELECT EXTERNAL_VERSION_ID
FROM REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTIONS
WHERE SUBSCRIPTION_NAME = 'ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION';

-- Did any scheduled jobs fail recently?
SELECT STATUS, ERROR_NUMBER, ERROR_MESSAGE, START_TIME, END_TIME
FROM M_REMOTE_REPLICATION_JOBS
WHERE REMOTE_SUBSCRIPTION_NAME = 'ABAP_SUBSCRIPTION'
ORDER BY START_TIME DESC;

 

Workload Management

Automatic replication jobs participate in SAP HANA's native admission control. You can define workload class thresholds to prevent replication from overwhelming the system:

CREATE WORKLOAD CLASS "AbapReplicationControl"
SET 'ADMISSION CONTROL REJECT CPU THRESHOLD' = '90',
'ADMISSION CONTROL QUEUE CPU THRESHOLD' = '70';

CREATE WORKLOAD MAPPING "AbapReplicationMapping"
WORKLOAD CLASS "AbapReplicationControl"
SET 'APPLICATION NAME' = '_SYS_REMOTE_REPLICATION_ABAP_DELTA_LOAD';

Jobs that exceed the QUEUE threshold are delayed; jobs that exceed the REJECT threshold fail with an error and are rescheduled.

 

Limitations to Know Before You Start

Before you commit to a schema design, check these constraints:

- Load behavior: Only Normal and Upsert are supported. INSERT and ARCHIVE load behaviors are not.  (Archived data in the source are converted to deletes and will be removed from the target table)
- Schema changes: The `WITH SCHEMA CHANGES` clause is not supported. If the CDS view schema changes, replication will fail and you'll need to manually update the target table and recreate the subscription.
- Initial load: Only the automated `QUEUE` command is supported. The manual `INITIAL LOAD PREPARE/EXECUTE/FINALIZE` flow is not available.
- Subquery expressions: Column projection and row filtering work. Function calls like `UPPER()` or arithmetic expressions in the subquery do not.
- Subscription query: Once created, the subscription query cannot be changed. `ALTER REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION … REFRESH DEFINITION` is not supported. Recreate it if you need to change the filter or projection.
- Target type: Only `TARGET TABLE` is supported. `TARGET PROCEDURE` and `TARGET TASK` are not.
- **NULL handling**: Empty values on the ABAP side (empty strings, zeros) may arrive as `NULL` in the target table. Account for this in downstream logic.

---

Summary

CDS view replication with the abapodbc adapter gives you a clean, CDC-based pipeline from SAP S/4HANA into SAP HANA Cloud. The lifecycle is straightforward: create the subscription, run the initial load with `QUEUE`, then choose between scheduled replication (`DISTRIBUTE`) or on-demand updates (`DELTA LOAD`). Built-in system views give you full visibility into job status, and native workload management ensures replication doesn't compete with your critical workloads.

The main things to keep in mind:

1. Truncate the target table before re-running the initial load — the system won't do it for you.
2. Schema changes on the source CDS view require a manual rebuild of the subscription.
3. Orphaned replications can silently drain ABAP resources — check with `DHADM` after any failed `CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION`.
4. Failed scheduled jobs are **not** automatically rescheduled — you need to re-issue `DISTRIBUTE` after resolving the error.

With that, you have everything you need to get replication running and keep it healthy in production.

entry.title: Dynamic height control of Tabstrip in classic Dynpro — is it possible?
entry.content:
Hi all,

I have a classic Dynpro with two subscreen areas at the top and a Tabstrip at the bottom. I want to hide one or both of the upper subscreen areas via a button, and have the Tabstrip grow to fill the freed space — basically a responsive layout.

The problem is that no matter what I change (height of the Tabstrip, height of the subscreen area inside it, height of the subscreens themselves), the Tabstrip always renders at the same size. Nothing seems to have any effect.

 

To make things worse, on smaller screens like a laptop display everything looks very cramped, and even when the upper areas are hidden the Tabstrip stays the same small size instead of expanding.

My setup:

  • Dynpro type: Normal
  • Two subscreen areas at the top (conditionally shown/hidden)
  • One Tabstrip at the bottom with a single subscreen area (TC_FICHAS_SCA) that loads different subscreens depending on the active tab
  • The Tabstrip contains a CL_GUI_ALV_GRID inside a Custom Container

 

My questions:

  1. Is there a hard limit on Tabstrip height in classic Dynpro that ignores whatever you set in Screen Painter?
  2. Is there any way to control the Tabstrip height dynamically at runtime (e.g. when hiding the upper areas)?

Any experience or workaround welcome. Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Step-by-Step Implementation Guide: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) Cash Flow Stateme
entry.content:
@daniel_wang I am currently configuring the Cash Flow Statement (Direct Method) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The structure (cash flow items and hierarchy, including parent and leaf nodes) has been completed, and the account assignments have also been maintained.

The relevant G/L accounts have been assigned as cash accounts, offsetting accounts, and invoice accounts according to the configuration model. End-to-end tests have been executed using standard processes (e.g., supplier invoice creation and outgoing payments via standard AP apps).

However, when running the report, the output (PDF) is completely empty, with no values displayed in any of the cash flow items.

I have verified that accounting documents are being posted correctly for the test scenarios, but the Cash Flow Statement still does not reflect any movements.

I would like to confirm:

  • What are the mandatory prerequisites for postings to be included in the Cash Flow Statement (Direct Method) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • What checks should be performed when the structure and account mapping are correctly maintained but the report still returns zero values?
  • Are there any additional critical configuration steps (e.g., version setup, derivation logic, or reconciliation settings) that could prevent data selection in the report?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Why OCM Is the Missing Piece in Every Organisation's AI Strategy
entry.content:
Why OCM Is the Missing Piece in Every Organisation's AI Strategy

There is a question being asked in boardrooms, team meetings, and coffee queues right now, and it is not going away: what is going to happen to my job?

Artificial intelligence is moving faster than most organisations anticipated, and the honest answer is that nobody has the full picture yet. What we do know is that the way work gets done is changing — and that the organisations navigating this well are not the ones with the biggest AI budgets. They are the ones that understood, early, that this was first and foremost a people challenge.

That is the core argument for Organisational Change Management in the AI era. Not as a box-ticking exercise bolted onto a technology rollout, but as the strategic discipline that determines whether AI investments actually deliver — or quietly gather dust while employees route around them.

Why This Wave Is Different

Previous automation largely addressed physical and transactional tasks. AI is operating on cognitive work — drafting, analysing, summarising, diagnosing, designing, deciding. The scope is broader, and the speed is faster, compressing adjustment timelines that previous technological transitions stretched across decades. Organisations that wait until the disruption is fully visible before acting will be managing a crisis rather than a change programme.

The core shifts are already underway. The proportion of time employees spend on repeatable execution is shrinking; the proportion spent on judgement, contextual reasoning, and human oversight is growing. Individual expertise is giving way to human-AI collaboration — producing outputs neither could generate alone. And stable role definitions are being replaced by a continuous adaptation imperative that makes learning culture a competitive necessity, not a nice-to-have.

SAP's approach to Business AI, embedded natively across its application suite, makes this shift tangible — surfacing AI capabilities directly within the workflows employees already use and providing organisations with a practical context for OCM conversations about what human-AI collaboration looks like in everyday work.

What OCM Actually Does Here

Done well, OCM is the systematic process of understanding what a change means for the people it affects and designing intentional support so they can move through it effectively. In an AI context, that means answering questions that technology roadmaps do not address.

What, specifically, is changing for each group of employees — not in aggregate, but at the level of which tasks shift, what success now looks like, and what remains unchanged? What are people actually afraid of — not just job loss, but fear of incompetence, irrelevance, or surveillance? Who needs to be involved in shaping the transition, not just receiving it? And what new capabilities are needed, and how will they actually be built?

On that last question, most AI change programmes fall shortest. A session on how to use a platform is not the same as building the judgement to work effectively alongside AI — knowing when to trust its outputs, when to probe them, and when to override them. SAP Learning Hub addresses this with dedicated AI literacy and SAP Business AI learning paths structured by role, going beyond tool familiarity to the broader context of working with AI responsibly. SAP Enable Now complements this with in-application, just-in-time guidance that makes capability building a continuous, workflow-embedded experience rather than a series of training events.

Governance, Ethics, and Risk

Strategy conversations about AI governance tend to stay at the policy level. What rarely receives enough attention is the gap between a governance document and the daily behaviour of thousands of employees making small AI-related decisions every hour. That gap is precisely where OCM operates.

Formal governance must address data privacy, algorithmic bias, regulatory compliance, and clear decision rights — who approves which AI uses, what escalation paths exist, and which decisions always require a human in the loop. OCM translates these frameworks into day-to-day behaviour by embedding responsible AI use in training, workflows, performance expectations, and cultural norms. It also surfaces the ethical questions employees are already encountering — outputs that feel wrong, datasets that seem skewed, automated steps that removed a human check for good reason — and routes those observations back to governance bodies before they become incidents.

SAP has published AI ethics principles spanning transparency, fairness, privacy, accountability, and reliability, which underpin how capabilities are built across the SAP portfolio. These give organisations deploying SAP Business AI a meaningful reference point for their own governance frameworks — grounded in how the technology was designed to behave. SAP's AI ethics commitments are reflected in its training and certification programmes, making responsible use a thread running through the learning experience, not an addendum.

Anchoring Change to Business Value

A change programme focused only on adoption rates and sentiment, without connecting to business outcomes, is vulnerable. Effective OCM anchors the programme to clearly defined outcomes from the outset — cost, quality, risk reduction, customer experience — with baselines defined before deployment so that improvement can be demonstrated, not just asserted.

A benefits map that traces the path from changed human behaviour to business outcome serves multiple purposes: it guides prioritisation, creates a common language between technology and business functions, and gives employees a meaningful answer to why this matters. Human metrics — confidence, sentiment, collaboration quality — should be tracked as leading indicators of those business outcomes, not as ends in themselves.

Workflow and Operating Model Redesign

There is a common failure mode in AI programmes that looks like success on the surface. The tool is deployed, training runs, adoption metrics look reasonable — and yet the efficiency gains never materialise. The diagnosis is almost always the same: AI was bolted onto an existing workflow rather than embedded into a redesigned one.

AI changes what is feasible at each step of a process, removing some bottlenecks and creating new ones in places that were not bottlenecks before. Operating model redesign — reconstructing how work flows, who owns which decisions, and where human review is mandatory — is a core OCM deliverable, not an afterthought. And it does not end at go-live. Frontline employees will discover, within weeks, edge cases the model handles inconsistently, steps where AI output increases cognitive load, and handoffs that create confusion because accountability was not clearly redesigned. The OCM infrastructure — change agents, feedback mechanisms, iterative review cycles — is what captures these insights and routes them back into ongoing refinement.

For organisations running SAP-based processes, Signavio's integration with the broader SAP landscape means workflow redesign is connected directly to the systems people use every day.

Structural Enablers

Individual change programmes are not sufficient when the underlying technology is evolving continuously. Organisations need structures that sustain ongoing adaptation.

An AI Centre of Excellence consolidates expertise, sets deployment standards, maintains governance, and coordinates learning across the organisation. For it to work, it must be closely partnered with the change function. An AI CoE without sustained attention to adoption and culture will underperform; OCM without a close relationship to the CoE will design programmes that drift from deployment realities. SAP Activate's OCM workstream provides a structured framework for exactly this collaboration — starting at the Discover phase, well before deployment, and designed for the pace and complexity of SAP-enabled transformation.

A change agent network and AI power user community are equally important. Peer influence is the most powerful driver of sustained behaviour change. Identifying respected individuals with strong AI working practices, investing in their confidence, and connecting them into a cross-organisational network creates a capability-building engine that outlasts any structured programme. SAP Enable Now and SAP Learning Hub together provide the platform and content infrastructure to make this network operationally effective at scale — including learning paths designed specifically for change practitioners navigating AI-led transformations.

Wellbeing, Performance, and the Conversation Organisations Are Avoiding

Some roles will be significantly reduced. For some individuals, the honest answer to "what happens to my job?" is not reassuring. Pretending otherwise — with upbeat messages about AI freeing people for higher-value work, without acknowledging genuine uncertainty — is communication employees see through immediately. When they do, trust collapses, and with it the psychological safety the programme depends on.

Wellbeing is also a concrete risk. AI can increase cognitive load in ways that are not immediately obvious — monitoring outputs for errors, managing the boundary between delegation and retention, recalibrating professional identity when the nature of work shifts. Surveillance anxiety is real. Burnout risk increases when people are learning new tools, adapting to new roles, managing uncertainty, and still being held to pre-AI performance standards. 

Performance measurement must also change. If systems punish the learning curve that comes with AI experimentation, the rational response is to stop experimenting. 

Beyond the Organisation

AI-driven changes do not stop at the organisation's boundary. Customers interact with AI-powered decisions — often without visibility into how AI shaped their experience. Suppliers face changed terms of relationship. Regulators, particularly under frameworks like the EU AI Act, increasingly require documented, auditable, explainable AI use. OCM principles — stakeholder mapping, clear communication, feedback mechanisms — apply to all of these audiences, not just to employees. SAP's investments in AI transparency and explainability features within its Business AI portfolio, and its published compliance documentation, give organisations working within SAP environments a concrete foundation for their regulatory engagement — reducing the burden of demonstrating responsible AI use to external authorities.

Where to Start

Six commitments define organisations that manage AI change effectively.

Anchor to value early. Define business outcomes and baselines before rollout. Map the path from behaviour change to business impact and track both. Start the OCM workstream at the technology evaluation stage, using SAP Activate guidance to structure the early-phase people work.

Redesign workflows, not just mindsets. Treat operating model redesign as a core programme deliverable, with iterative post go-live refinement built in. Use SAP Signavio to make this continuous and data-driven.

Embed governance behaviourally. Translate ethics and risk frameworks into daily practice through training, performance expectations, and cultural norms. Draw on SAP's AI ethics principles and SAP Learning Hub's responsible AI curriculum to ground this in the technology employees are actually using.

Build structural capability. Establish the AI CoE partnership and the change agent network. Deploy SAP Enable Now and SAP Learning Hub to make the network effective at scale.

Protect wellbeing and rethink performance. Monitor cognitive load and burnout risk actively. Redesign metrics so the system rewards the experimentation it needs. Use SAP SuccessFactors to operationalise both commitments.

Extend beyond the organisation. Apply the same rigour to customer, supplier, and regulatory communication that you apply internally.

The technology will keep advancing regardless. The organisations that genuinely benefit are those that treat AI as a sociotechnical challenge — connecting it to clear outcomes, embedding governance in culture, redesigning operations with the people who use them, and building the institutional capability to keep adapting.

For organisations in the SAP ecosystem, the combination of SAP Activate, SAP Enable Now, SAP Learning Hub, SAP Signavio, and SAP SuccessFactors means that the infrastructure for effective AI change management is already available. The question is whether it is deployed with the same seriousness as the AI capabilities it supports.

The choice belongs entirely to the organisation. And it starts with how seriously it takes change management.

entry.title: GCS JWT token generation - SAP CPI neo - PKIX path building failed:
entry.content:
We have a requirement to upload files from SAP CPI (Neo environment) to Google Cloud Storage. As part of this integration, we implemented an OAuth 2.0 JWT Bearer flow using a Google service account.

The integration flow has been designed as follows:

  • The service account private key is stored securely in the CPI Keystore (converted to .p12 format).
  • A Groovy script retrieves the private key from the keystore and generates a signed JWT.
  • The generated JWT is then used in an HTTP adapter call to obtain an access token from Google’s OAuth token endpoint.
  • The access token is intended to be used in a subsequent HTTP call to upload files to Google Cloud Storage.

However, we are encountering an error when invoking the OAuth token endpoint:

https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token

The error message observed is:

java.net.ConnectException: PKIX path building failed: sun.security.provider.certpath.SunCertPathBuilderException: unable to find valid certification path to requested target

We have already imported the relevant Google/DigiCert certificates (including GTS Root R1 and WR2, as well as DigiCert Global Root certificates) into the CPI Neo keystore. Despite this, the PKIX error persists during the HTTPS call.

Could you please advise what might be missing or incorrectly configured in this scenario? Additionally, are there any known limitations or special considerations in SAP CPI Neo when establishing HTTPS connections to Google OAuth endpoints (oauth2.googleapis.com)?

Your guidance on how to resolve this issue, or confirmation of any platform-related constraints, would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: API for post goods receipt for handling unit with inbound delivery reference in warehouse missing
entry.content:
With warehouse inbound delivery API: https://api.sap.com/api/WAREHOUSEINBOUNDDELIVERY_0001/overview it is not possible to post goods receipt for handling unit one by one.

Please suggest the correct approach for this


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: API for post goods receipt for handling unit with inb...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, when dealing with warehouse inbound deliveries, it is not possible to post goods receipt for handling units one by one using the Warehouse Inbound Delivery API. This API does not support individual handling unit goods receipt postings related to an inbound delivery.

Key Points:

  • Handling Units: These are physical units consisting of packaging material and the goods contained within.
  • Warehouse Management: In a WM-managed storage location, goods receipt for handling units must be posted through the highest-level handling unit APIs for Logistics Cross Topics - Post Goods Receipt in WM-Managed Storage Location in the hierarchy.

Example:

To post goods receipt for an inbound delivery linked to a handling unit, you must use the highest-level handling unit:

POST <host>/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_handlingunit/srvd_a2x/sap/handlingunit/0001/HandlingUnit(HandlingUnitExternalID='800036410',Warehouse='1050')/SAP__self.PostGoodsReceipt?sap-client=<client>

This approach ensures that the goods receipt is processed correctly for the entire hierarchy of handling units associated with the inbound delivery.

Please let us know by there if you have further questions about your question! We keep at your service.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: 財務諸表バージョンや利益センタグループの有効期間での表示内容確認
entry.content:
SAP Machine Translation Portal translation:

See what appears in the validity period of a financial statement version or profit center group

This will be a detailed review of the SR:637765/2026 responses created previously.
The following is a description of the detailed check:
SR:637765/2026:
In the Trial Balances app, financial statement versions are not supported as report hierarchies.
- The app uses only the account hierarchy in General Ledger Accounting.

What does it mean that the above part doesn't support it?
*You can specify the financial statement version as a hierarchy in the trial balance.

As an example, we currently assume that the Financial Statement Version: AAAA Validity Period: (1) 1/2025~12/31/2025, (2) 1/1/2026~12/31/9999, both have the status (1) and (2) Active, so this status: Only one active in the financial statement version can be output in the active version.
